coredata.dc:title: Use of ultrasound irradiation during acid etching of the 2024 aluminum alloy: Effect on corrosion resistance after anodization
coredata.dc:creator: Moutarlier, V.
Viennet, R.
Gigandet, M.P.
Hihn, J.Y.
coredata.dc:description: While aluminum alloys are widely used in industrial applications, their protection by anodization as surface treatment always requires a preparation step by alkaline or acid etching. In this paper, use of ultrasound during the acid etching step on the 2024 aluminum alloy was investigated. Etching rate, calculated as of weight loss, was measured under ultrasound irradiation, and compared to silent conditions. The etched surface was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD). Surface treatment was performed up to the final anodization step samples, and their final properties were evaluated as a function of various pre-treatments, including acid etching under ultrasound. The main evaluation concerned anticorrosion properties through electrochemical tests: polarization measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in NaCl solution. Finally, use of ultrasound irradiation during acid preparation induced a beneficial effect on the corrosion performance of the anodic layer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2024 aluminum alloy
Sulfuric-nitric-ferric acid etching
Power ultrasound

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: 16th Meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of ultrasound effects on phases: Grazing incidence X-ray diffraction
Study of particles removal mechanisms by SEM and EDS observation

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Equipment for the etching process under power ultrasound irradiation.

Grazing incidence XRD patterns of 2024 aluminum alloy surface a) before pre-treatment, b) after acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min in silent conditions and c) under ultrasound irradiation (20 kHz, 70 W).
SEM micrograph of 2024 aluminum alloy surface before pre-treatment. Zones A1 and A2: Al-Cu-Mg particles - zones B1 and B2: Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si inclusions.

SEM micrograph of 2024 aluminum alloy surface after acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min, in silent conditions. Zones A’ Al-Cu-Mg particles - zones B’: Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si inclusions – zones C’: holes after particles removal.
SEM micrograph of 2024 aluminum alloy surface after acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min, under ultrasound irradiation (20 kHz, 70 W).

Representation in cross section of the reactivity of the 2024 intermetallic particles a) in acid etching: dissolution of Al-Cu-Mg anodic phase and dissolution of aluminum matrix around Al-Cu and Al-Cu-Mn-Fe-Si phases, b) in acid etching under ultrasound irradiation (US): significant removal of particles.
Polarization curves of anodic layers formed on 2024 aluminum alloy surface without preparation and after acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min, without and with ultrasound irradiation (20 kHz, 70 W).
Nyquist plots for anodic layers formed on 2024 aluminum alloy surface prepared in acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min, in silent conditions and under ultrasound irradiation (20 kHz, 70 W).
Equivalent circuit for modeling impedance behavior of anodic layer.

Etching rate of the 2024 aluminum alloy after 10 min of immersion in sulfuric/nitric/ferric acid solution, without and with ultrasound irradiation. Distance variation between aluminum alloy and ultrasonic source = 1 cm.

Etching rate of the 2024 aluminum alloy after 10 min of immersion in sulfuric/nitric/ferric acid solution at 50 °C, under ultrasound. Variation of the distance between aluminum alloy and ultrasonic source.
EDS analysis of 2024 aluminum alloy surface before pre-treatment, according Fig. 3.
EIS parameters of anodic layers formed on 2024 aluminum alloy surface prepared in acid etching at 50 °C, during 10 min, in silent conditions and under ultrasound irradiation (20 kHz, 70 W).

head.ce:title: Use of ultrasound irradiation during acid etching of the 2024 aluminum alloy: Effect on corrosion resistance after anodization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UTINAM UMR 6213 CNRS Univ. Bourgogne Franche Comte, 16 route de Gray, 25030 Besançon, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: UTINAM UMR 6213 CNRS Univ. Bourgogne Franche Comte

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UTINAM UMR 6213 CNRS Univ. Bourgogne Franche Comte, 16 route de Gray, 25030 Besançon, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While aluminum alloys are widely used in industrial applications, their protection by anodization as surface treatment always requires a preparation step by alkaline or acid etching. In this paper, use of ultrasound during the acid etching step on the 2024 aluminum alloy was investigated. Etching rate, calculated as of weight loss, was measured under ultrasound irradiation, and compared to silent conditions. The etched surface was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM/EDS) and X-Ray Diffraction (XRD). Surface treatment was performed up to the final anodization step samples, and their final properties were evaluated as a function of various pre-treatments, including acid etching under ultrasound. The main evaluation concerned anticorrosion properties through electrochemical tests: polarization measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in NaCl solution. Finally, use of ultrasound irradiation during acid preparation induced a beneficial effect on the corrosion performance of the anodic layer.

ce:section.ce:para: The 2024 aluminum alloy is widely used in aerospace applications (wings, fuselage outer skin) due to its high strength to weight ratio and high damage tolerance [1,2]. The ultimate tensile strength and the yield strength of pure aluminum are about 290 MPa and 10 MPa, whereas 2024 aluminum alloy rise up to 430 MPa and 280 MPa, respectively. These attractive mechanical properties are explained by the heterogeneous microstructure of this alloy due to the presence of copper and magnesium as the principal alloying elements. 2024 aluminum alloy is composed of heterogeneous microstructures, which consist of a combination of hardening precipitates, dispersoids and intermetallic particles within the aluminum solid solution. Hardening precipitates and dispersoids are very small in size (around 100–400 nm), whereas intermetallic phases exhibit bigger sizes (1–20 µm). Buchheit et al. have identified several particles for the 2024-T3 aluminum alloy and two major types of intermetallic particles are mentioned [3]. Due to the presence of magnesium in this alloy, the intermetallic particles Al-Cu-Mg (S phase) with Al2CuMg composition, are detected. When the Cu/Mg ratio is small in these particles, the composition similar to Al2Cu (θ phase) can be found. Other intermetallic particles such as the Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si-type, whose the compositions varied greatly, are distributed through the matrix. The Al-Cu-Mg phases are regular (typically round) particles, while the Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si-type is irregular.

Although showing favorable mechanical properties due to the presence of these heterogeneities, the 2024 aluminum alloy is relatively susceptible to localized corrosion [4–6] and generally requires surface treatment such as anodization [7,8], in practical applications. In anodizing process, an anodic film is generated to improve corrosion performance of aluminum alloy. During the anodic film growth, initially compact barrier layer is formed on the surface, following by formation of a porous layer.

Before anodization, the surface of the alloy must be prepared. The quality of this pre-treatment is essential to ensure good corrosion resistance of the anodic film. Surface preparation results in oxidation of the alloy surface by dissolution of aluminum and alloying element species. Usually, pre-treatment consists in alkaline or acid etching to remove natural oxide and particles on the alloy surface [9–12]. As aluminum is amphoteric, it could effectively be dissolved in acid (sulfuric, nitric, chromic acids) or alkaline (NaOH) solutions.

The most common type of acid etching is based on sulfuric-chromic acid. However, use of hexavalent chromium is unadvisable from a health and environmental point of view since it is toxic and carcinogenic (European REACH regulation for example). Thus, new classes of environmentally-friendly pre-treatment processes are proposed to substitute sulfuric-chromic etching: nitric/sulfuric acids with ferric sulfate [13,14]. One of the principles of this pre-treatment is the oxidation of copper from the matrix and the particles, by ferric ions. This ensures a decrease of copper content at the top of the alloy surface.

Removal of copper during the etching process could be an important point since this alloying element reacts during the anodizing process, and thus modifies anodic film formation. Indeed, many papers have shown that copper presence in alloy matrix and in particles generates defects in the anodic film, while also disrupting its growth [15–17]. During anodization, alloy/film interface enriches in non-valve metal (copper in this case) through preferential oxidation of aluminum. When a critical concentration of this alloying element is reached, co-oxidation of both aluminum and copper occurs. Oxygen evolution, related to oxidation of the previous transition metal, is generated, resulting in disruption of anodic film growth and reduction of film formation efficiency. Copper contained in particles also induces oxygen evolution, causing alteration of anodic film morphology [17].

Several studies have shown that ultrasound has a great interest for surface treatment applications such as control of the structural shape of the surface [18,19] or improving of the efficiency of surface preparation [20]. Other works have investigated the use of power ultrasound for etching of aluminum or others substrates. Some of them show that ultrasound is a good alternative to conventional acid treatments and improve cleaning treatment methods [21–24], or are focused on the increasing of the oxidation reaction rate [25,26]. The possible mechanism could be an action on copper oxidation by providing a local energy supply.

The objective of this study was to examine the influence of power ultrasound irradiation during the acid pre-treatment of 2024 aluminum alloy. The dissolution of the aluminum alloy was investigated by the determination of the etching rate in silent condition and in ultrasound presence.

Ultrasound is known to induce cavitation phenomena in liquid media: the cavitation activity results in asymmetric bubble collapse, directing micro-jets towards the surface, whereas inertial bubble collapse in the surface vicinity generates shock waves. The associated effects consist of chemical (free radical generation), thermal and mechanical effects. Lot of works have investigated the influence of operating parameters such as the distribution of cavitation activity in the reactor, the variation of distance between transducer and the surface as well as the specific effect of frequencies [27,28]. These effects are mainly generated by shock waves and micro-jets with high velocity. These micro-jets are generated by implosion of cavitation bubbles near a surface and they could strike the surface with velocity over 100 m/s [29,30]. Moreover, the values of Sherwood’s numbers are very high close to the transducer [28] which means it is better to reduce the distance between the transducer and the surface. It is generally recognized for both effects that when frequency increases, the mechanical effect decreases but the chemical effect increases. Frequencies used for cleaning are generally around 20–40 kHz (mechanical effect) [17,31,32]. For this study, mechanical effects and improving of mass transfer were privileged, and experiments under ultrasound are carried out at 20 kHz.

Composition and morphology of the etched surface were characterized by X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) and Scanning Electron Microscopy/Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (SEM/EDS). The final efficiency of acid etching under ultrasound presence was proofed after anodizing. The corrosion resistance of anodic layers after various pre-treatments was evaluated by electrochemical tests: polarization measurements and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) in NaCl solution.

Etching rate was determined from weight loss. For a sample with an area A (cm2), a density ρ (g/cm3), an etching time t (min), and weights m1 and m2 (g) before and after etching, the etching rate R (nm/min) was given by R = 107 (m1 – m2)/(A.ρ.t). Density of aluminum was ρ = 2.8 g/cm3 and active area was A = 3.14 cm2. In each case, three samples were used to confirm good reproducibility.

Grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance Bruker diffractometer, with a Cu Kα radiation. The grazing incidence method was employed to study surfaces as X-ray penetration was limited. The incidence angle was fixed at a small value (in this study the grazing angle is 0.5°) and the angle between the incident beam and the diffracted beam varied: 2θ angle scanned from 25° to 70°. Under these conditions, analysis depth was controlled by the grazing angle and remained constant during sweeping. For Cu radiation on aluminum alloy with a grazing angle of 0.5°, the calculated absorption (Photoelectric + Compton + Rayleigh) indicated that the penetration depth for the diffracted beam was 1.5 µm. All XRD measurements were carried out within 24 h after pre-treatments treatments to avoid the pollution of the sample.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) methods were used to study the aluminum alloy surface before and after pre-treatments. Measurements were also carried out within 24 h after pre-treatment for the same reason, with the JEOL JSM microscope model.
Surface roughness was estimated by stylus profilometer (Dektak 150, Veeco) with a resolution of 10 nm (scan length of 1000 µm, for 300 s). Measurements were carried out directly after etching.

To evaluate the impact of ultrasound presence during the acid etching step, corrosion behavior of the anodized sample was observed. Anodization process was performed at 20 °C, for 10 min, by applying 20 V, in a 180 g/L sulfuric acid solution. The thickness of the anodic film was about 5 µm. Corrosion behavior of anodic film was generally studied in neutral solution containing pitting agents such as chloride ions. Standard solution was composed of NaCl with concentration close to the salinity of seawater (30–50 g/L). Thus, corrosion tests were performed in 5% NaCl solution at room temperature [6,35]. Corrosion resistance of anodic film was evaluated by electrochemical tests: polarization measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), with a classical three-electrode cell [36,37]. The anodic layer with a 1 cm2 surface was used as the working electrode. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum (Pt) wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. For polarization measurements, potential was scanned at a rate of 1 mV/s, from −700 mv to −100 mV/SCE and the simultaneously developed current was recorded. The current–potential curves (potentiodynamic polarization curves) were analyzed. Impedance measurements were carried out in the 105 to 10−2 Hz frequency range and signal amplitude was 10 mV, with a Parstat 2273 frequency response analyzer. Samples were immersed for 1 h before impedance measurements (time required to stabilize the electrode potential) and EIS data were presented as Nyquist plots. The impedance behavior of the anodized samples was simulated with a suitable equivalent electrical circuit ZSimWin software program and the various impedance parameters were evaluated. All electrochemical tests were conducted 48 h after anodizing. Four replicates for each test were examined.

Etching rate in sulfuric/nitric/ferric acid combination was estimated after 10 min’ immersion of 2024 aluminum alloy samples, with and without ultrasound irradiation, at 25 °C and 50 °C. Effect of distance between sample and ultrasonic source on etching rate was also examined. Distance varied from 5 cm to 1 cm. All data were shown in Tables 2 and 3
. The small standard deviation indicates a good reproducibility of the results.

The influence of use of ultrasound during the etching process is beneficial. Etching rates significantly increased in presence of ultrasound for both temperatures tested. At 50 °C, values rise from 85 to 145 nm/min when the ultrasound device was switched on. Thus, aluminum dissolution greatly improved during acid etching. Moreover, the beneficial effect of ultrasound was obtained when the sample was close to the ultrasound source (1 cm) as expected. For larger distances, etching rates were similar to silent conditions (about 90 nm/min). As observed in previous works [27,28], this distance corresponds to the point of the bubbles clouds, which corresponds to an optimum between the maximum of cavitation activity and the minimum of bubble disturbance. Thus, the horn to sample distance was fixed at 1 cm for all experiments.

The implementation of ultrasound imparts mechanical energy which helps the etching process, possibly thanks to mechanical effects and/or chemical ones. The latest may influence the etching rate of these alloys by oxidation of the metallic species due to the water hydrolysis, which reaction are likely mass transport limited. To decouple the chemical and mechanical effects, control data have been provided by a sample exposed to sonication without chemical etchants. As no enhancement of the dissolution rate were observed in this case, the global etching is much more likely attributed to a combination of the acid attacks which dissolved the oxide layer assisted by mechanical vibration of the hard precipitates, which facilitates their removal. This effect is generated by cavitation bubbles which grow and collapse near the surface, contributing then to the beneficial liberation of high energy with local heating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of ultrasound effects on phases: Grazing incidence X-ray diffraction
ce:section.ce:para: To examine composition at the top surface of the 2024 aluminum alloy, grazing incidence diffraction was used. Fig. 2 shows the grazing incidence XRD data of the 2024 alloy before acid etching, after preparation without and with ultrasound irradiation. The incidence angle was fixed (0.5°) to analyze, for each sample, the same depth of the top surface (1.5 µm).

The significant peaks correspond to the α-Al matrix. The α phase refers to the solid solution of copper and other alloying elements in the FCC (face centered cubic) lattice of aluminum [1]. Other peaks could be associated with intermetallic particles presented in 2024 aluminum alloy: Al-Cu(-Mg) (Al2CuMg and Al2Cu phases) and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn particles [1,3]. Also, our peak identification well tallies with previous works such as M. Gao et al. [38] that analyzed the crystal structure of 2024 alloy particles. The Al2CuMg particles (S phase) are determined as an orthorhombic structure (a = 0.400 nm, b = 0.925 nm, c = 0.715 nm). The Al2Cu particles (θ phase) have a body-centered tetragonal structure (a = 0.605 nm and c = 0.487 nm). And, the Al-Cu-Fe-Mn particles are identified as having a rhombohedral structure (a = 1.985 nm, c = 9.022 nm). θ and S phases are predominant compared to Al-Cu-Fe-Mn particles. Literature indicates that Al-Cu-Mg particles are those most often observed in the 2024 alloy (60% of particles are Al-Cu-Mg particles) [38,39].

XRD data for etched samples show a decrease in intensity of particle peaks, whereas intensity of aluminum peaks remains constant. During etching process in silent conditions, alloy particles clearly react. The intensity of the θ and S phases decreases indicating a probable removal of Al-Cu-(Mg) particles from the surface. Moreover, the decrease of all particle peaks is more pronounced in ultrasound presence. This could imply an extensive removal of particles at the top surface of the 2024 aluminum alloy with the use of ultrasound irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of particles removal mechanisms by SEM and EDS observation
ce:section.ce:para: Microstructural characterization of aluminum alloys usually employs scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Fig. 3 and Table 4 present the SEM/EDS measurements for 2024 aluminum alloy. As already mentioned, the microstructure of this alloy is complex. These analysis allow distinguishing: Al-Cu-Mg particles (zones A1 and A2) and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si inclusions (zones B1 and B2). Al-Cu-Mg particles are usually round in shape and could be several microns in diameter. Some Al-Cu-Mg particles contain significant amounts of Mg, and their measured composition perfectly tallies with the stoichiometry of the Al2CuMg phase (zone A1 for example). Others contain a small quantity of Mg and could be associated with Al2Cu (zone A2 for example). There are substantial amounts of iron-, copper-, manganese and silicon-containing aluminum intermetallic consistent particles in the alloy. These Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si inclusions are large and irregular in shape.

Cavities and defects were observed on the alloy surface after acid preparation as can be seen in Fig. 4 (in silent conditions). This could be explained by the reactivity of particles during the acid etching process. Indeed, Al2CuMg, Al2Cu and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si particles react in aggressive media such as acid solution. The reactivity of particles has been investigated: Al2CuMg phases acting as an active phase, while Al2Cu and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn-Si acting as noble phases [1].

The Al2CuMg phases are anodic relative to the aluminum matrix (due to the presence of magnesium). Previous works revealed that the potential of the Al2CuMg phase is 300–400 mV, more negative than the aluminum potential [4,39–42]. Thus, during immersion in acid medium, the Al2CuMg phase and the aluminum matrix form a micro-galvanic cell where the Al2CuMg phase acts as anode and aluminum matrix as cathode. Because the cathode is much larger than the anode, the Al2CuMg particles are expected to dissolve rapidly, leaving micro-pits on the surface. The circular holes with a diameter of around 1–2 µm observed on the surface may be linked to this preferential dissolution of anodic Al2CuMg phase (example: areas A’ in Fig. 4).

The Al-Cu-Mn-Fe-Si and Al-Cu (Al2Cu) phases are cathodic relative to the aluminum matrix. Thus, micro-galvanic cells may be generated between the anodic aluminum matrix and the cathodic phases, implying the dissolution of aluminum matrix around particles. Some works specified that the attack around the Al-Cu-Mn-Fe-Si particles was less severe than around the Al-Cu particles [4,39–40]. Examples of the cathodic phase reactivity are observed in Fig. 4 with areas B’: aluminum matrix dissolved around the cathodic particle. Moreover, the attack around the cathodic particles could lead to their removal from the surface, with the emergence of holes as illustrated by area C’ in Fig. 4.

Fig. 5 presents the alloy surface after acid etching under ultrasound. Phase activation is clearly more significant under ultrasound (Fig. 5) rather than under silent conditions (Fig. 4). The different states of 2024 phase’s activation (dissolution of anodic particles or aluminum dissolution around cathodic particles) are not visualized for surfaces prepared under ultrasound irradiation. Its effect during the etching process results in a substantial removal of all types of particles, as large holes (from 5 to 10 µm in diameter) were observed on aluminum alloy surfaces etched under ultrasound irradiation. In the same manner, surface roughness (Ra values) increased from 230 ± 20 nm for the initial alloy to 305 ± 5 nm after acid preparation and to 350 ± 10 nm under ultrasound irradiation. These results confirm the increase in roughness after acid etching due to the removal of particles, particularly in presence of ultrasound.

Reactivity of intermetallic particles have occurred in acid solution as described above and as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. It is important to note that in solutions without aggressive agent, ultrasound irradiation could not activate the galvanic mechanism. Application of sonication improves the galvanic mechanism initiated by the etchant agent (acid solution). Representation of reactivity of particles in acid etching in presence of ultrasounds is schematized in Fig. 6b: ultrasound irradiation allows removing significantly particles from the alloy surface. This could be due to the erosion induces by acoustics cavitation [43]. Its mechanical effects are principally generated by shock waves and micro-jets with high velocities: these factors could be responsible for surface erosion with the removal of particles.

Some authors have observed an erosion of aluminum foil by acoustic cavitation from 20 kHz to 4880 kHz [44–46]. At lower frequencies, surface modification occurs due to the abrasive action of energetic micro-jetting. This leads to significant changes in surface morphology and some damage could be observed. At higher frequencies, surface morphology is more homogeneous. The more ultrasound frequency increases, the more mechanical effects decrease [27,28]. The maximum bubble radius, just before collapse, and the jet velocity generated by the collapse of cavitation bubbles decreases as frequency increases. Ultrasonic conditions employed during this work (20 kHz) indicated no specific damage on the 2024 alloy surface (Fig. 5).

The efficiency of the etching process was tested after anodizing. Anodic layers were elaborated on the 2024 alloy after different surface preparations. Electrochemical tests in NaCl solution were employed to characterize the corrosion performance of these anodic layers.

Fig. 7 shows anodic and cathodic curves for the anodic films without surface preparation, and generated after acid etching in silent conditions as well as under ultrasound irradiation. The “noise” of the anodic polarization curves corresponds to the repeated formation and dissolution of unstable pits. Data have revealed a decrease in cathodic and anodic courants for anodic films prepared in acid solution, which was more pronounced under ultrasound irradiation. The corrosion current density (jcorr) was 315µA/cm2 for anodic film without surface preparation, which drops to 100 and to 6µA/cm2 for prepared anodic films without and with ultrasound, respectively. Pitting potential (Epitt) was also modified. The lowest pitting potential corresponds to anodic layers without surface preparation (about −550 mV/SCE). Pitting potential values increase when the aluminum alloy is prepared before anodizing: about −390 mV/SCE for an acid etching in silent conditions and about −300 mV/SCE under ultrasound irradiation. The presence of contaminant, natural oxide and intermetallic particles on the untreated alloy surface is known to reduce the corrosion performance of the post-treatment [18]. It can also be concluded that an efficient pre-treatment is mandatory to improve corrosion resistance of anodic films, and, as the lowest corrosion current density and the highest passive region are observed in ultrasound presence, the efficiency of this technology.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) could be used to confirm the positive effect of the use of ultrasound during the etching step. Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 8 .

The anodic layer consists of a very thin compact layer and a thick outer porous layer. To describe the electrochemical behavior of this system, both layers are considered independent and separated into two phases: a barrier layer and a porous layer. The properties of each layer could be characterized by resistances and capacitances, in parallel and in series. The model in Fig. 9 is successfully applied to explain the properties of the barrier and porous layers [40]. In this model, Rel corresponds to electrolytic resistance (20 Ω.cm2), Rp to porous layer resistance, Cp to porous layer capacitance, Rp to barrier layer resistance, and Cb to barrier layer capacitance. The capacitive behavior of the barrier and porous layers was better simulated by constant phase elements (CPE) than by a pure capacitance due to their inhomogeneity. CPE is most often used to describe the frequency dependence of non-ideal capacitive behavior. The impedance of a CPE (ZCPE) is given by ZCPE = 1/(C(jω)n) where C corresponds to the capacitance, and parameter n is the frequency dispersion factor which depends on different factors such as surface roughness and varies from 0 to 1. The more homogeneous is the surface, the smoother it is, and thus the closer the value of parameter n is to 1. When n is equal to 1, the CPE parameter can be considered to be an ideal capacitance.

The porous layer could not be characterized when the oxide film is unsealed due to the high conductivity of the pores which are filled with the brine solution. Therefore, only one capacitive loop is observed in the low frequency range that is related to the barrier layer. Barrier parameters are interesting to compare with respect to corrosion behaviors since the barrier layer plays a significant role in preventing the electrolyte ions from diffusing into the substrate.

A good corrosion performance is associated with a high value of barrier resistance and a low value of barrier capacitance. Indeed, a capacitance with lower capacity consumes less electrical current to be charged. Current means the flow of electrons via charge transfer in aluminum corrosion. When a capacitance is charged, no more current will pass through it. Thus, the sooner a capacitance is charged, the sooner the through current will decrease. Table 5 indicates barrier parameters of unsealed anodic layers formed after the different pre-treatments. Barrier resistance increases from 430 to 580 kΩ.cm2, while barrier capacitance decreases from 0.77 to 0.48µF/cm2, by the addition of ultrasound during the pre-treatment. It can be concluded that the corrosion performance of the barrier layer increases for pre-treatment with ultrasound.

Moreover, the increase in parameter n indicates the effective role of ultrasound in increasing the homogeneity of the barrier layer. The capacitance Cb could be connected to the barrier layer thickness d by the equation: Cb = ε0εrA/d, where ε0 = 8.85 × 10−14 F/cm is the dielectric constant of vacuum, εr = 10 is the relative constant for alumina, and A is the electrode surface area. This equation is acceptable when parameter n is close to 1, i.e. the CPE is close to an ideal capacitance. Parameters n are effectively close to 1, with 0.91 in silent conditions and 0.98 in presence of ultrasound. The calculated thicknesses of the barrier layer for unsealed anodic films formed after the different pre-treatments are reported in Table 5. The positive role of ultrasound during the etching step is also revealed by the increase in barrier layer thickness.

The enhancement of corrosion performance for anodic film prepared in presence of ultrasound could be explained by the significant removal of particles (Al2Cu, Al2CuMg and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn particles). Many papers have shown that the presence of particles induced the formation of defects in the anodic layer and disrupted its growth [15–17]. Indeed, the difference in potentials between the intermetallic particles and the aluminum matrix results in uneven current distribution at the growing alloy/film interface during the anodizing process. As for acid etching under ultrasound irradiation, a reduced number of intermetallic particles leads to more stable anodic film growth, thus improving its corrosion resistance.

Acid etching (sulfo-nitro-ferric acid) of the 2024 aluminum alloy was conducted in presence of ultrasound irradiation at a 20 kHz frequency. The use of ultrasound irradiation has clearly showed an increase in etching rates. XRD data showed the elimination of particles at the surface and SEM analyses confirming a large removal of Al2Cu, Al2CuMg and Al-Cu-Fe-Mn particles from the alloy surface.

The application of ultrasound irradiation improves the acid etching mechanism by mechanical effects of acoustic cavitation, through shock waves and micro-jets. Even if the alloy surface becomes extremely rough, the corrosion performance of post-treatment (anodizing) is enhanced. This is ascribed to the increase in surface potential homogeneity, in direct relationship with the elimination of galvanic cells between particles and the aluminum matrix. Using ultrasound during the etching step could be a way of using lower acid concentrations, as their beneficial effects could compensate the decrease in solution aggressiveness.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of 2024–T3: an aerospace aluminum alloy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: ]: Z. Huda, N. Taib, T. Zaharine, “Characterization of 2024-T3: an aerospace aluminum alloy”, Materials Chemistry and Physics 113 (2009) 515-517

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exfoliation corrosion of friction stir welded dissimilar 2024-to-7075 aluminum alloys

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: ]: P.L. Niu, W.Y. Li, N. Li, Y.X. Xu, D.L. Chen, “Exfoliation corrosion of friction stir welded dissimilar 2024-to-7075 aluminum alloys” Materials Characterization 147 (2019) 93-100

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Local dissolution phenomenon associated with S phase (Al2CuMg) particles in aluminum alloy AA 2024-T3

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: ]: R.G Buchheit, R.P. Grant, P.F. Hilava, B. McKenzie, G.L. Zender, “Local dissolution phenomenon associated with S phase (Al2CuMg) particles in aluminum alloy AA 2024-T3”Journal of electrochemistry Society, 144 (1997) 2621-2628

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Localized corrosion of 2024 T3 aluminum alloy in chloride media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: ]: V. Guillaumin, G. Mankowski, “Localized corrosion of 2024 T3 aluminum alloy in chloride media”, Corrosion Science 41 (1999) 421-438

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stable pit formation on AA2024-T3 in NaCl environment

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Corrosion inhibition of 2024–T3 aluminum alloy in 3.5% NaCl by thiosemicarbazione derivates
